title: U.S. indicts former Cuban president Raúl Castro, escalating Trump pressure campaign
full_text:
U.S. indicts former Cuban president Raúl Castro, escalating Trump pressure campaign
The Latest
- The U.S. Justice Department has announced criminal charges against former Cuban president Raúl Castro, boosting President Donald Trump's campaign against Havana's one-party government.
- Castro, 94, was charged in connection with an incident in 1996, during which Cuban jets shot down exiled civilians' planes.
- Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel pushed back on U.S. claims that his country's communist government was to blame for Cubans' hardships, noting U.S. sanctions and an energy blockade are still in place.
- Trump has pushed for a change of government in the island nation and, after the capture of then-Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro, warned Cuba was "next". But the U.S. president wouldn't say whether Castro's indictment would lead to further escalation.
Updates
May 20
Kevin MaimannWhat to know about Raúl Castro's indictment
On Wednesday the U.S. Justice Department announced criminal charges against former Cuban president Raúl Castro, 94, and several others, in connection with a 1996 incident in which Cuban jets shot down two planes carrying exiled civilians, killing four.
U.S. officials, including Trump, would not say how, or when, the charges will be enforced.
Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said he expects Castro will "show up" in the U.S., "by his own will or by another way."
But Trump brushed off a question about whether he'd launch an operation to extricate Castro like he did with Venezuela's Nicolás Maduro.
"I don't wanna say that," he told reporters.
Trump has pushed for regime change in Cuba, and warned Cuba was "next" after Maduro's capture in January.
Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel pushed back following claims by U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio that the suffering of the Cuban people is the fault of the country's communist government.
Díaz-Canel blamed the suffering on U.S. blockades, saying the "collective punishment" is "becoming an act of genocide."
While Cuban Americans welcomed the move to indict Castro, residents in Havana stood by their government and expressed a willingness to fight back against any American incursion.
You can follow our continuing coverage of this story at cbc.ca/news.
- Kevin Maimann
'Makes no sense': Cubans in Havana criticize U.S. move
In Havana on Wednesday, residents condemned the U.S. indictment of former Cuban leader Raúl Castro, who was charged in connection with a 1996 incident in which Cuban jets shot down two civilian planes carrying exiles, killing four. 'At that time, they had their defence and their right to defend their airspace,' said Rosmery Pena, a legal adviser for self-employed workers.
In Havana, Cubans who spoke with AFP supported Castro and expressed a willingness to fight back against the U.S.
"It makes no sense at all," said Rene Sierra, a 64-year-old construction worker. "They [the U.S.] are not going to come in here or do anything, because we'll be waiting for them. We are not Venezuela. Here, everyone knows how to shoot, from children to adults. Let them come — we'll destroy everything."
Agustin Sanabria, 76, who retired from the Cuban Ministry of Justice, also said the indictment makes no sense.
"They think they can do the same thing they did to Maduro, but Maduro was betrayed internally. But here, that is not going to happen," he said.
Legal adviser Rosmery Pena, 65, said Castro and others working with him had a right to defend Cuba's airspace at the time when the planes were shot down.
"If their airspace was violated, they can take the measure they consider appropriate to prevent it from happening again. That is my view," she said.
— With files from AFP
- Kevin Maimann
'Right and very fair': Cuban Americans praise indictment
Cubans in Miami reacted on Wednesday to the U.S. Justice Department announcing criminal charges against former Cuban leader Raúl Castro. 'The people have suffered greatly.... They need freedom, and for those who must go down to go down,' said writer Francys Fabelo.
Cuban Americans in Miami praised the announcement of Castro's indictment.
"I think the indictment is right and very fair. All of us Cubans have been waiting for this for over 60 years," said writer Francys Fabelo, 67, who added that "many people" would like to see Castro suffer.
"You don’t mess with President Trump. I think he means business," she said.
Adrien Rodriguez, 26, said the indictment is "the first step to indict the whole Cuban Communist Party."
"This is very good. It's never too late for justice."
Anthony Trugilllo, 56, said he hopes negotiations follow today's announcement.
"I believe you cannot just go in there with helicopters and pull Raúl Castro out. Besides, I don’t think it will do any good," he said. "He's already 94 years old."
— With files from AFP
- Kevin Maimann
What's life like for Cubans at the moment?
While Cuba has long been struggling and has faced sanctions for decades, a U.S. fuel blockade and energy embargo launched in January sharpened the suffering of its residents.
Cubans are facing rolling blackouts — sometimes lasting more than 20 hours at a time — leading to thousands of delayed medical procedures and difficulty accessing, storing and cooking food. Fuel shortages mean people also lack transportation to get around, including to work.
John Kirk, a former Latin American studies professor at Dalhousie University who has written numerous books on Cuba, calls the Trump administration's policy on Cuba "death by a thousand cuts."
He told CBC News these "psychological operations," in addition to government actions like the indictment of Castro, are meant to wear down popular resistance and incite an overthrow of the government.
"All these things are building in a crescendo," he said.
- Mike Crawley
Trump says it's 'a big day'
U.S. President Donald Trump hailed his government's indictment of former Cuban leader Raúl Castro on Wednesday, calling it 'a very big moment' for Cuban Americans and people who want to return to the country.
Speaking to reporters this past hour, Trump said the indictment is "big news" that Cuban Americans appreciate.
"It's a very big day, very important day," the U.S. president said on the tarmac at Joint Base Andrews near Washington, D.C.
When asked if he'd take further steps to up the pressure on the Cuban regime, Trump didn't bite.
"There won't be escalation. I don't think there needs to be. Look, the place is falling apart. It's a mess," he said.
Trump then brushed off a question about whether he'd launch an operation to extricate Castro like he did with Venezuela's Nicolás Maduro.
"I don't wanna say that," he said.
- Kevin Maimann
Were the planes shot down in 1996 really on a 'humanitarian mission?'
U.S. officials speaking this afternoon referred to the Brothers to the Rescue as innocent civilians on a "humanitarian mission," who were victims of an "unprovoked attack" by Cuba.
Mark Entwistle, who served as Canada's ambassador to Cuba in 1996 and was in Havana when the shoot-down happened, told CBC News that while the incident was a tragedy, that framing misses some important context.
Leading up to the shoot-down, the Miami-based Brothers to the Rescue did multiple low-level flyovers above the Malecón promenade in Havana, dropping anti-Cuban government leaflets. The Cuban government warned the group and the U.S. to stop the incursions into its airspace or risk escalation.
At the time, Cuba was facing violent attacks from other U.S.-based groups of Cuban exiles, like Alpha 66 and Omega 7, who were effectively "anti-Castro terrorist groups," Entwistle said.
The Cuban government considered Brothers to the Rescue a terrorist organization and told the UN they had proof that the group had planned violent attacks.
On Feb. 24, 1996, three unarmed planes carrying Brothers to the Rescue members entered a zone just north of Havana. Two of the planes were shot down by Cuban fighter jets, killing all four people aboard. The third plane, carrying the organization’s leader, narrowly escaped.
"The whole incident sits in a bigger context of a long history of violation of Cuban sovereignty and attacks on Cuban national territory by groups that are not at all innocent," Entwistle said.
— With files from The Associated Press
- Mike Crawley
What is a grand jury indictment?
The U.S. grand jury system for indictments differs from how similar charges are laid in Canada.
A jury panel of at least 16 members meets in closed-door sessions to hear evidence presented by government attorneys, including witness testimony.
The grand jury's job is to decide whether there is "probable cause" to believe the accused committed the crime alleged by the prosecution. At least 12 grand jury members must vote for indictment for a charge to move forward to trial.
Castro was indicted by a grand jury in April, but the indictment was only made public today.
Federal prosecutors have discretion over when they unseal an indictment, in case law enforcement needs to put in place a plan to arrest an accused person who might flee.
In Canada, the decision of whether there's enough evidence to move forward in court on serious charges is up to Crown prosecutors.
- Kevin Maimann
Blanche says Castro will be brought to the U.S.
Acting U.S. attorney general Todd Blanche was asked Wednesday to comment on the timing of the indictment against former Cuban leader Raúl Castro and what steps, if any, the U.S. was prepared to take to bring the 94-year-old to the U.S.
"This isn't a show indictment," Blanche said.
He said Castro was indicted to be brought to the U.S. to "face justice" in front of a jury, though he didn't offer details on how that might happen.
"We expect that he will show up here, by his own will or by another way."
- Verity Stevenson
U.S. announcement held on Cuban Independence Day
At the news conference announcing the charges against Castro and others — held on the anniversary of Cuba's independence from Spain, May 20, 1902 — Blanche said it was important for the event to be held in Florida.
"The community here, you all, understands the history of the Cuban regime better than anyone in America," Blanche said. "Many families here know the cost of oppression."
The crowd cheered and clapped as Blanche and other U.S. officials lauded the work of the Trump administration.
Castro, now 94, was Cuba's defence minister at the time of the 1996 downing of the civilian planes operated by Brothers to the Rescue, a Miami-based exile group.
-With files from The Associated Press
- Kevin Maimann
Officials say they never let go of 1996 case
U.S. officials say they never stopped pursuing evidence to press charges over the last three decades.
"We'll keep pursuing anyone who intends to harm our citizens and they will face American justice," FBI deputy director Christopher Raia said.
Florida Senator Ashley Moody said it was "never a cold case" and thanked investigators for "keeping it hot."
